topic.what: Characterisation of gastric microbiome changes across the stages of the Correa cascade (superficial gastritis, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, gastric cancer), revealing progressive enrichment of oral bacteria
topic.how: Cohort study using 16S rRNA gene sequencing of gastric biopsies from patients at each Correa cascade stage, with microbial co-occurrence network analysis and differential abundance testing
topic.why_matters: Demonstrates that gastric carcinogenesis is accompanied by systematic microbiome remodelling with increasing dominance of oral-origin bacteria, suggesting microbial dysbiosis as a potential contributor to or biomarker for cancer progression

characteristics.dimension: oral bacteria enrichment across carcinogenesis stages
characteristics.what_it_measures: Progressive increase in oral-origin bacterial taxa from superficial gastritis to gastric cancer
characteristics.value: significant enrichment of Peptostreptococcus, Streptococcus, Parvimonas, Prevotella, and Rothia in cancer vs gastritis
characteristics.vs_baseline: distinct from H. pylori-dominated microbiome in superficial gastritis
characteristics.evidence: 16S rRNA sequencing with LEfSe differential abundance analysis
characteristics.context: Cross-sectional cohort; causal direction of microbiome changes not established

good_for: understanding microbiome shifts in gastric carcinogenesis
hypothesis generation for microbial contributions to cancer
requires: well-phenotyped patient cohorts across Correa stages

novelty: First systematic mapping of microbiome composition across all four Correa cascade stages
Identification of oral-origin bacteria as progressively enriched taxa in gastric carcinogenesis
Microbial co-occurrence network disruption as a feature of advanced disease stages
open_problems: Causal vs consequential role of microbiome changes in carcinogenesis is unknown
Longitudinal studies tracking microbiome evolution within individuals are needed
Functional contribution of enriched oral bacteria to tumour microenvironment is unexplored
Whether microbiome-based biomarkers can outperform existing screening approaches
